## Features

[](#features)

* Portable (_Only needs a web browser at runtime, WebView is optional_)
* Single header file
* Lightweight (_Few Kb library_) & Small memory footprint
* Fast binary WebSocket communication protocol
* Multi-platform & Multi-Browser
* Uses private profile for safety
* Cross-platform WebView (_Optional_)

## UI & The Web Technologies

[](#ui--the-web-technologies)

[Borislav Stanimirov](https://ibob.bg/) discusses using HTML5 in the web browser as GUI at the [C++ Conference 2019 (_YouTube_)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bbbcZd4cuxg).

[![CPPCon](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/34311583/265778796-4e830caa-4ca0-44ff-825f-7cd6d94083c8.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..n6Ytemzihn8tdlcvlCiM0r5BuDb4EMElhz_rQj2YJkk)](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/34311583/265778796-4e830caa-4ca0-44ff-825f-7cd6d94083c8.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..n6Ytemzihn8tdlcvlCiM0r5BuDb4EMElhz%5FrQj2YJkk)

Web application UI design is not just about how a product looks but how it works. Using web technologies in your UI makes your product modern and professional, And a well-designed web application will help you make a solid first impression on potential customers. Great web application design also assists you in nurturing leads and increasing conversions. In addition, it makes navigating and using your web app easier for your users.

### Why Use Web Browsers?

[](#why-use-web-browsers)

Today's web browsers have everything a modern UI needs. Web browsers are very sophisticated and optimized. Therefore, using it as a GUI will be an excellent choice. While old legacy GUI lib is complex and outdated, a WebView-based app is still an option. However, a WebView needs a huge SDK to build and many dependencies to run, and it can only provide some features like a real web browser. That is why WebUI uses real web browsers to give you full features of comprehensive web technologies while keeping your software lightweight and portable.

### How Does it Work?

[](#how-does-it-work)

[![Diagram](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/34311583/265780957-dbde3573-3161-421e-925c-392a39f45ab3.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..mphJA8-pDCBhGc3oVp9N1XvZaL9mEjDJEE-CRW5HV88)](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/34311583/265780957-dbde3573-3161-421e-925c-392a39f45ab3.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..mphJA8-pDCBhGc3oVp9N1XvZaL9mEjDJEE-CRW5HV88)

Think of WebUI like a WebView controller, but instead of embedding the WebView controller in your program, which makes the final program big in size, and non-portable as it needs the WebView runtimes. Instead, by using WebUI, you use a tiny static/dynamic library to run any installed web browser and use it as GUI, which makes your program small, fast, and portable. **All it needs is a web browser**.

## Minimal WebUI Application

[](#minimal-webui-application)

* **C**  
#include "webui.h"  
int main() {  
  size_t my_window = webui_new_window();  
  webui_show(my_window, "<html><head><script src=\"webui.js\"></script></head> Hello World ! </html>");  
  webui_wait();  
  return 0;  
}
* **C++**  
#include "webui.hpp"  
#include <iostream>  
int main() {  
  webui::window my_window;  
  my_window.show("<html><head><script src=\"webui.js\"></script></head> C++ Hello World ! </html>");  
  webui::wait();  
  return 0;  
}

# Python-WebUI v2.5.8

[](#python-webui-v258)

[![](https://camo.githubusercontent.)](https://github.com/webui-dev/python-webui/pulse) [![](https://camo.githubusercontent.)](https://github.com/webui-dev/python-webui/releases/latest) [![](https://camo.githubusercontent.)](https://github.com/webui-dev/python-webui/blob/main/LICENSE)

> Use any web browser as GUI, with Python in the backend and HTML5 in the frontend, all in a lightweight Python package.

[![Screenshot](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/webui-dev/webui-logo/main/screenshot.png)](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/webui-dev/webui-logo/main/screenshot.png)

## Features

[](#features)

* Portable (_Needs only a web browser at runtime_)
* Lightweight (_Few Kb library_) & Small memory footprint
* Fast binary communication protocol
* Multi-platform & Multi-Browser
* Using private profile for safety
* Original library is written in Pure C

## Installation

[](#installation)

`pip install webui2`

## Minimal Example

[](#minimal-example)

from webui import webui

my_window = webui.Window()
my_window.show('<html><script src="webui.js"></script> Hello World! </html>')
webui.wait()

[More examples](https://github.com/webui-dev/python-webui/tree/main/examples)

## Documentation

[](#documentation)

* [Online Documentation](https://webui.me/docs/#/python%5Fapi)

## CppCon 2019 Presentation

[](#cppcon-2019-presentation)

[Borislav Stanimirov](https://ibob.bg/) explained at [C++ Conference 2019 (_YouTube_)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bbbcZd4cuxg) how beneficial it is to use the web browser as GUI.

[![ScreenShot](/webui-dev/python-webui/raw/main/img/cppcon_2019.png)](/webui-dev/python-webui/blob/main/img/cppcon%5F2019.png)

## UI & The Web Technologies

[](#ui--the-web-technologies)

Web application UI design is not just about how a product looks but how it works. Using web technologies in your UI makes your product modern and professional, And a well-designed web application will help you make a solid first impression on potential customers. Great web application design also assists you in nurturing leads and increasing conversions. In addition, it makes navigating and using your web app easier for your users.

## Why Use Web Browser?

[](#why-use-web-browser)

Today's web browsers have everything a modern UI needs. Web browsers are very sophisticated and optimized. Therefore, using it as a GUI will be an excellent choice. While old legacy GUI lib is complex and outdated, a WebView-based app is still an option. However, a WebView needs a huge SDK to build and many dependencies to run, and it can only provide some features like a real web browser. That is why WebUI uses real web browsers to give you full features of comprehensive web technologies while keeping your software lightweight and portable.

## How does it work?

[](#how-does-it-work)

[![ScreenShot](/webui-dev/python-webui/raw/main/img/webui_diagram.png)](/webui-dev/python-webui/blob/main/img/webui%5Fdiagram.png)

Think of WebUI like a WebView controller, but instead of embedding the WebView controller in your program, which makes the final program big in size, and non-portable as it needs the WebView runtimes. Instead, by using WebUI, you use a tiny static/dynamic library to run any installed web browser and use it as GUI, which makes your program small, fast, and portable. **All it needs is a web browser**.

Web application UI design is not just about how a product looks but how it works. Using web technologies in your UI makes your product modern and professional, And a well-designed web application will help you make a solid first impression on potential customers. Great web application design also assists you in nurturing leads and increasing conversions. In addition, it makes navigating and using your web app easier for your users.

## Why Use Web Browser?

[](#why-use-web-browser)

Today's web browsers have everything a modern UI needs. Web browsers are very sophisticated and optimized. Therefore, using it as a GUI will be an excellent choice. While old legacy GUI lib is complex and outdated, a WebView-based app is still an option. However, a WebView needs a huge SDK to build and many dependencies to run, and it can only provide some features like a real web browser. That is why WebUI uses real web browsers to give you full features of comprehensive web technologies while keeping your software lightweight and portable.

## How does it work?

[](#how-does-it-work)

[![ScreenShot](/webui-dev/python-webui/raw/main/img/webui_diagram.png)](/webui-dev/python-webui/blob/main/img/webui%5Fdiagram.png)

Think of WebUI like a WebView controller, but instead of embedding the WebView controller in your program, which makes the final program big in size, and non-portable as it needs the WebView runtimes. Instead, by using WebUI, you use a tiny static/dynamic library to run any installed web browser and use it as GUI, which makes your program small, fast, and portable. **All it needs is a web browser**.

## WebUI

[](#webui)

WebUI is a lightweight, open-source library that lets you build cross-platform desktop applications using web technologies (_HTML, CSS, and JavaScript_). Unlike frameworks that bundle a built-in browser, WebUI uses the user’s existing installed browser as the app’s graphical interface (GUI). WebUI was originally created by [Hassan Draga](https://github.com/hassandraga) and is now maintained by the open-source community. It supports backend languages such as [C/C++](https://github.com/webui-dev/webui), [Zig](https://github.com/webui-dev/zig-webui), [Python](https://github.com/webui-dev/python-webui), [Go](https://github.com/webui-dev/go-webui), [Bun](https://github.com/webui-dev/bun-webui), [Deno](https://github.com/webui-dev/deno-webui), and more through wrappers.

[![Screenshot](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/webui-dev/webui-logo/main/screenshot.png)](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/webui-dev/webui-logo/main/screenshot.png)

[](#python-webui-v258)

> Use any web browser as GUI, with Python in the backend and HTML5 in the frontend, all in a lightweight Python package.

## Features

[](#features)

* Portable (_Needs only a web browser at runtime_)
* Lightweight (_Few Kb library_) & Small memory footprint
* Fast binary communication protocol
* Multi-platform & Multi-Browser
* Using private profile for safety
* Original library is written in Pure C

## Documentation

[](#documentation)

pip install webui2

[](#example)

from webui import webui

MyWindow.show('<html><script src="webui.js"></script> Hello World! </html>')
webui.wait()

## WebUI

[](#webui)

WebUI is a lightweight, open-source library that lets you build cross-platform desktop applications using web technologies (_HTML, CSS, and JavaScript_). Unlike frameworks that bundle a built-in browser, WebUI uses the user’s existing installed browser as the app’s graphical interface (GUI). WebUI was originally created by [Hassan Draga](https://github.com/hassandraga) and is now maintained by the open-source community. It supports backend languages such as [C/C++](https://github.com/webui-dev/webui), [Zig](https://github.com/webui-dev/zig-webui), [Python](https://github.com/webui-dev/python-webui), [Go](https://github.com/webui-dev/go-webui), [Bun](https://github.com/webui-dev/bun-webui), [Deno](https://github.com/webui-dev/deno-webui), and more through wrappers.

[![Screenshot](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/webui-dev/webui-logo/main/screenshot.png)](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/webui-dev/webui-logo/main/screenshot.png)

A WebView option was added recently as an additional alternative.

[![Screenshot](https://github.com/webui-dev/webui/raw/main/examples/C/frameless/webui_frameless.png)](https://github.com/webui-dev/webui/blob/main/examples/C/frameless/webui%5Fframeless.png)

# Lua/WebUI Framework Plan

## Overview

You're building a **Lua binding for WebUI2** - a lightweight library that uses the user's installed browser as GUI instead of bundling one. This is similar to what already exists in Python, Go, Zig, etc.

---

## Similar Projects Reference

### In Other Languages (official WebUI wrappers):
| Language | Repository | Design Pattern |
|----------|------------|----------------|
| **Python** | `webui-dev/python-webui` (362 ⭐) | OOP - `webui.Window` class |
| **Go** | `webui-dev/go-webui` (265 ⭐) | OOP - `window.Window` struct |
| **Zig** | `webui-dev/zig-webui` (805 ⭐) | OOP - `webui.Window` struct |
| **Nim** | `webui-dev/nim-webui` (159 ⭐) | OOP - `WebUI` object |
| **Deno/JS** | `webui-dev/deno-webui` (371 ⭐) | OOP - class-based |

### Existing Lua Solutions (NOT WebUI):
- **`zhaozg/lua-webview`** - Uses `webview/webview.h` (tiny library, different from WebUI)
- **`javalikescript/lua-webview`** - Another webview binding
- **`MeowBoy326/Webview-Lua`** - Windows WebView2 only

---

## Proposed Lua API Design

### 1. Core Module Structure

```lua
-- webui.lua - Main module
local webui = require("webui")

-- Module-level constants
webui.version  -- "2.5.0"
webui.Chrome   -- 2
webui.Firefox  -- 3
webui.Edge     -- 4
webui.Deno     -- 1 runtime
webui.NodeJS   -- 2 runtime

-- Global functions
webui.new_window()           -- returns window_id
webui.wait()                 -- block until all windows close
webui.wait_async()           -- non-blocking, returns true if windows open
webui.exit()                 -- close all windows
webui.clean()                -- free memory

-- Configuration
webui.set_config(option, boolean)  -- show_wait_connection, ui_event_blocking, etc.
webui.set_timeout(seconds)   -- set connection timeout
```

### 2. Window Class

```lua
local Window = {}
Window.__index = Window

-- Constructor
function Window.new()        -- or webui.Window()
function Window.new_id(id)   -- create with specific ID

-- Show window
Window:show(content)                    -- HTML, URL, or file
Window:show_browser(content, browser)   -- force specific browser
Window:show_wv(content)                 -- use WebView instead of browser

-- Window control
Window:close()         -- hide (keep in memory)
Window:destroy()       -- free completely
Window:focus()         -- bring to front
Window:minimize()      -- (WebView only)
Window:maximize()      -- (WebView only)
Window:set_kiosk(bool) -- fullscreen
Window:set_size(w, h)
Window:set_position(x, y)
Window:set_center()
Window:set_resizable(bool)   -- (WebView only)
Window:set_hide(bool)
Window:set_frameless(bool)    -- (WebView only)
Window:set_transparent(bool)  -- (WebView only)

-- Server control  
Window:set_root_folder(path)
Window:set_profile(name, path)
Window:set_proxy(url)
Window:set_public(bool)       -- allow network access

-- Query
Window:is_shown()        -- returns bool
Window:get_url()         -- returns string
Window:get_port()        -- returns number
Window:get_hwnd()        -- returns lightuserdata (platform-specific)
Window:browser_exist(browser) -- returns bool
```

### 3. Event Binding System

```lua
-- Bind Lua function to JS
local bind_id = Window:bind(element_name, callback)

-- Callback receives Event object
--[[
Event = {
    window = window_id,
    event_type = "connected" | "disconnected" | "mouse_click" | 
                 "navigation" | "callback",
    element = "element_id",      -- HTML element that triggered
    bind_id = number,
    client_id = number,
    connection_id = number,
    -- argument getters
    get_count(),
    get_string([index]),
    get_int([index]),
    get_float([index]),
    get_bool([index]),
    get_size([index])  -- bytes
}
]]

-- Return values to JS
Window:return_int(event, value)
Window:return_float(event, value)  
Window:return_string(event, value)
Window:return_bool(event, value)

-- Context (user data attached to bind)
Window:set_context(element, data)
Window:get_context(event)  -- returns user data

-- Also support interface-style binding (simpler for wrappers)
webui.bind(window_id, element_name, callback)
```

### 4. JavaScript Execution

```lua
-- Fire-and-forest
Window:run("script")           -- all clients
Window:run_client(event, "script")

-- With response (single client only)
local ok, result = Window:script("script", timeout_seconds)
local ok, result = Window:script_client(event, "script", timeout)

-- Raw binary data
Window:send_raw(func_name, data_string, size)
Window:send_raw_client(event, func_name, data, size)
```

### 5. Navigation & URL

```lua
Window:navigate(url)              -- all clients
Window:navigate_client(event, url)
webui.open_url(url)               -- open in default browser
```

### 6. Runtime & Environment

```lua
Window:set_runtime(runtime)  -- Deno, NodeJS, Bun, None
Window:set_custom_browser_args("--remote-debugging-port=9222")

-- Utility
webui.encode(str)      -- base64 encode
webui.decode(str)     -- base64 decode
webui.get_mime_type(filename)
webui.get_best_browser(window_id)
webui.is_high_contrast()

-- TLS/SSL
webui.set_tls_certificate(cert_pem, key_pem)
```

---

## JavaScript API (webui.js usage)

The Lua framework should also provide/distribute a JS bridge that frontend developers use:

```javascript
// Available in webui.js (standard WebUI JS)
webui.bind('myButton', function(event) {
    // Call Lua callback
});

// Or use the generated callbacks
function myLuaCallback(event) {
    // Get data from event
    let data = event.detail;  // JSON
    
    // Return to Lua
    event.response = "ok";
}

// Alternative: call Lua directly
webui.call('myFunction', {arg1: 'value'});

// Receive from Lua
webui.on('myEvent', function(data) {
    console.log(data);
});
```

---

## Implementation Approach

### Option 1: Pure Lua + FFI (LuaJIT or Lua 5.3+)
```lua
-- Load the C library via FFI
local ffi = require("ffi")
ffi.load("webui-2")
-- Bind C functions manually
```

**Pros**: Pure Lua, no C compilation needed for wrapper, works with LuaJIT  
**Cons**: FFI can be tricky for callbacks, no native error handling

### Option 2: C Extension Module (Lua binding)
```c
// webui.c - Lua C module
#include "lua.h"
#include "lauxlib.h"
#include "webui.h"
// Bind all functions
```

**Pros**: Full control, native Lua experience, proper error handling  
**Cons**: Need to compile per-platform, more work upfront

### Recommendation: Start with **Option 1 (FFI)** for prototype, then Option 2 for production.

---

## Project Structure

```
lua-webui/
├── README.md
├── LICENSE (MIT)
├── .github/
│   └── workflows/
│       └── test.yml
├── src/
│   ├── webui.lua           -- Main Lua module (OO wrapper)
│   └── webui_bindings.lua  -- FFI bindings (or generated)
├── examples/
│   ├── minimal.lua
│   ├── callbacks.lua
│   └── full_example.lua
├── test/
│   ├── test_basic.lua
│   └── test_callbacks.lua
├── Makefile / CMakeLists.txt  -- Build WebUI lib
└── rockspec (if using LuaRocks)
```

---

## Implementation Phases

### Phase 1: Core Foundation
- [ ] Set up project structure
- [ ] Build/compile WebUI C library
- [ ] Create FFI bindings for core functions
- [ ] Implement `Window` class basic methods
- [ ] Basic show/close functionality

### Phase 2: Events & Callbacks
- [ ] Implement `bind(callback)` system
- [ ] Handle event structure parsing
- [ ] Implement return values (int, float, string, bool)
- [ ] Add context management

### Phase 3: JavaScript Interop
- [ ] Implement `run()` and `script()`
- [ ] Add raw data sending
- [ ] Test JS ↔ Lua communication

### Phase 4: Window Management
- [ ] Size, position, kiosk, etc.
- [ ] Navigation and URL handling
- [ ] Multi-window support

### Phase 5: Polish
- [ ] Documentation
- [ ] Examples
- [ ] Tests
- [ ] Release / rockspec

---

Before proceeding, I'd like your input:

1. **Target Lua version** - LuaJIT required, or support plain Lua 5.3+ with FFI too?
   
2. **API style preference**:
   - `webui.new_window()` → returns object with methods?

3. **Naming convention**:

   - `webui.window` (lowercase)
   - `webui.Window.new()`

4. **Scope ambition**:
   - Direct WebUI C binding only?
   - Or also include higher-level UI helpers (HTML builders, etc.)?

# Lua WebUI Framework - Full Implementation Plan

## Table of Contents

1. [Executive Summary](#executive-summary)
2. [Why This Project](#why-this-project)
3. [How It Works](#how-it-works)
4. [Technical Architecture](#technical-architecture)
5. [API Design](#api-design)
6. [Implementation Roadmap](#implementation-roadmap)
7. [Pain Points & Challenges](#pain-points--challenges)
8. [Things To Keep In Mind](#things-to-keep-in-mind)
9. [Alternatives Considered](#alternatives-considered)

---

## Executive Summary

This document outlines the full implementation plan for creating a Lua/LuaJIT binding for **WebUI2** - a lightweight C library that uses the user's installed web browser as a GUI instead of bundling one.

**Target**: LuaJIT (FFI-based binding)
**License**: MIT  
**Module Name**: `webui`  
**Primary API Style**: `webui.Window.new()` (Love2D-style)

---

## Why This Project

### 1.1 The Problem

- Lua has limited native GUI options
- Existing solutions are either:
  - **Platform-specific** (IUP, Tk)
  - **Complex to embed** (Qt binding, FLTK)
  - **Heavy** (Electron wrappers)
  - **Abandoned** (many Lua GUI projects)
- No modern, lightweight, cross-platform GUI library for Lua

### 1.2 Why WebUI?

| Feature | WebUI | Electron | Qt | Tauri |
|---------|-------|----------|----|----|
| Binary Size | **~1MB** | ~150MB | ~50MB | ~10MB |
| Runtime | Browser only | Chromium + Node | Qt runtime | WebView2 |
| Portable | **Yes** | Yes | No | Yes |
| Wrapper Exists | 17+ languages | N/A | Yes | Rust only |

**Key Advantages**:
- Uses existing browser (no bundled runtime)
- Cross-platform out of the box
- Very small memory footprint
- Modern UI with HTML/CSS/JS
- Active community (4.1k GitHub stars)
- Multiple language wrappers already exist

### 1.3 Why Not Just Use Existing Lua webview?

Existing options:
- `zhaozg/lua-webview` - Uses `webview/webview.h` (different, simpler library)
- `javalikescript/lua-webview` - Also bindings to `webview/webview.h`
- `MeowBoy326/Webview-Lua` - Windows WebView2 only

**Why create new?**
- None bind to **WebUI2** (more feature-rich)
- WebUI2 has better cross-browser support
- WebUI2 includes WebSocket communication
- No complete Lua binding exists for WebUI2
- We can make it idiomatic Lua (Love2D-style API)

---

## How It Works

### 2.1 WebUI Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Your Lua Application                  │
│                                                          │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │              lua-webui Module                   │   │
│   │              (FFI Bindings)                     │   │
│   └────────────────────┬────────────────────────────┘   │
│                        │                                 │
│   ┌────────────────────▼────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │            WebUI2 C Library                      │   │
│   │         (libwebui-2.a / .so)                    │   │
│   └────────────────────┬────────────────────────────┘   │
│                        │                                 │
│              ┌────────┴────────┐                       │
│              │   WebSocket      │                       │
│              │   Connection     │                       │
│              └────────┬────────┘                       │
└────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┘
                         │
        ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐
        │                │                │
        ▼                ▼                ▼
   ┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐
   │ Chrome  │     │ Firefox │     │  Edge   │
   └─────────┘     └─────────┘     └─────────┘
        │                │                │
        └────────────────┼────────────────┘
                         ▼
              ┌─────────────────────┐
              │   webui.js         │
              │   (Browser Bridge)  │
              └─────────────────────┘
```

### 2.2 Communication Flow

1. **Lua → Browser**: `Window:run("alert('hello')")`
   - Sends JS via WebSocket
   - Browser executes immediately

2. **Browser → Lua**: `webui.bind('btn', callback)`
   - User clicks element
   - Browser sends event via WebSocket
   - Lua callback invoked with event data

3. **Two-way**: `Window:script("return 1+1", 5)` → returns "2"

### 2.3 Implementation Method

**Choice**: LuaJIT FFI (Foreign Function Interface)

```lua
local ffi = require("ffi")
ffi.load("webui-2")  -- Load native library
```

**Why FFI over C Extension**:
- No compilation step for the Lua wrapper
- Easier distribution (pure Lua + prebuilt .so/.dll)
- Faster iteration/prototyping
- LuaJIT is already a requirement
- Can convert to C extension later if needed

---

## Technical Architecture

### 3.1 Project Structure

```
coconut-milk/
├── coconut-milk-scm-1.rockspec    -- LuaRocks spec
├── Makefile                        -- Build WebUI C lib
├── src/
│   ├── webui.lua                   -- Main module
│   ├── webui_bindings.lua          -- FFI declarations (auto-generated)
│   └── webui_constants.lua         -- Browser/runtime constants
├── lib/
│   ├── webui.h                     -- WebUI header (submodule)
│   └── webui.js                    -- Browser bridge (copied)
├── examples/
│   ├── 01_minimal.lua
│   ├── 02_buttons.lua
│   └── 03_full.lua
└── test/
    └── test.lua
```

### 3.2 Module Hierarchy

```
webui (module)
├── .version = "2.5.0"
├── .Chrome = 2
├── .Firefox = 3
├── .Edge = 4
├── .Deno = 1
├── .NodeJS = 2
│
├── newWindow()          → Window
├── wait()
├── waitAsync()         → boolean
├── exit()
├── clean()
├── setConfig(option, boolean)
├── setTimeout(seconds)

├── decode(base64)      → str
├── getMimeType(file)   → string
├── getBestBrowser(id)  → browserId
├── isHighContrast()    → boolean
│
└── Window (class)
    ├── new()           → Window instance
    ├── newId(id)       → Window with specific ID
    │
    ├── show(content)
    ├── showBrowser(content, browser)
    ├── showWv(content)
    │
    ├── close()
    ├── destroy()
    ├── focus()
    ├── minimize()
    ├── maximize()
    ├── setKiosk(bool)
    ├── setSize(w, h)
    ├── setPosition(x, y)
    ├── setCenter()
    ├── setResizable(bool)
    ├── setHide(bool)
    ├── setFrameless(bool)
    ├── setTransparent(bool)
    │
    ├── setRootFolder(path)
    ├── setProfile(name, path)
    ├── setProxy(url)
    ├── setPublic(bool)
    ├── setRuntime(runtime)
    │
    ├── isShown()       → boolean
    ├── getUrl()        → string
    ├── getPort()       → number
    ├── getHwnd()       → lightuserdata
    │
    ├── bind(element, callback)  → bindId
    ├── returnInt(event, value)
    ├── returnFloat(event, value)
    ├── returnString(event, value)
    ├── returnBool(event, value)
    │
    ├── run(script)
    ├── runClient(event, script)
    ├── script(script, timeout)       → (ok, result)
    ├── scriptClient(event, script)   → (ok, result)
    │
    ├── sendRaw(funcName, data, size)
    ├── sendRawClient(event, funcName, data, size)
    │
    └── navigate(url)
```

---

## API Design

### 4.1 Design Principles

1. **Love2D-style**: `webui.Window.new()` returns a class instance
2. **Lua idioms**: Use `:` for methods, `.` for constants
3. **Snake case**: Methods like `set_root_folder` → Lua: `setRootFolder` or keep snake_case?
   - Decision: **CamelCase** to match Love2D convention
4. **Error handling**: Return `nil, error` or use pcall for FFI calls
5. **FFI safety**: Validate all pointers before dereferencing

### 4.2 Method Naming Convention

| C Function | Lua Method |
|------------|------------|
| `webui_new_window` | `Window.new()` |
| `webui_show` | `Window:show(content)` |
| `webui_wait` | `webui.wait()` |
| `webui_bind` | `Window:bind(element, callback)` |

### 4.3 Event Handling

```lua
-- The callback receives an "event" object
Window:bind("myButton", function(event)
    -- event.window       (number)
    -- event.eventType    (string) 
    -- event.element      (string)
    -- event.bindId       (number)
    -- event.clientId     (number)
    
    -- Get arguments
    local count = event:getCount()
    local name = event:getString(0)
    local age = event:getInt(1)
    
    -- Return value
    Window:returnString(event, "Hello " .. name)
end)
```

---

## Implementation Roadmap

### Phase 1: Foundation (Week 1)

- [ ] Set up project with LuaRocks
- [ ] Add WebUI as git submodule or download
- [ ] Create rockspec file
- [ ] Build WebUI C library
- [ ] Create FFI bindings for 20 core functions:
  - `webui_new_window`, `webui_show`, `webui_wait`
  - `webui_close`, `webui_destroy`
  - `webui_bind`, `webui_return_*`
  - etc.

### Phase 2: Basic Window (Week 2)

- [ ] Implement `Window` class
- [ ] Test `show("Hello World")`
- [ ] Test `webui.wait()`
- [ ] Test multiple windows
- [ ] Test HTML file serving

### Phase 3: Events (Week 3)

- [ ] Implement callback system
- [ ] Parse webui_event_t struct
- [ ] Implement argument getters
- [ ] Test bind/return flow

### Phase 4: JavaScript (Week 4)

- [ ] Implement `run()` and `script()`
- [ ] Implement raw data sending
- [ ] Test JS ↔ Lua round-trip

### Phase 5: Polish (Week 5)

- [ ] Window controls (size, position, kiosk)
- [ ] Navigation methods
- [ ] Error handling
- [ ] Documentation
- [ ] Examples (minimum 3)

---

## Pain Points & Challenges

### 5.1 Technical Challenges

#### Challenge 1: FFI Callback Registration

**Problem**: WebUI uses C function pointers for callbacks. FFI can't directly call Lua functions.

**Solution**: 
```lua
-- Use C wrapper that handles the bridge
local function c_callback_wrapper(event_ptr)
    -- Convert C event to Lua table
    -- Look up Lua callback from global registry
    -- Call Lua function
    -- Set return value if needed
end

-- Register with WebUI
ffi.C.webui_bind(window_id, element, cast(callback_type, c_callback_wrapper))
```

#### Challenge 2: Event Parsing

**Problem**: `webui_event_t` is a C struct. Reading it via FFI requires exact layout matching.

**Solution**: Define exact FFI structs:
```lua
ffi.cdef([[
typedef struct {
    size_t window;
    size_t event_type;
    char* element;
    size_t event_number;
    size_t bind_id;
    size_t client_id;
    size_t connection_id;
    char* cookies;
} webui_event_t;
]])
```

#### Challenge 3: String Handling

**Problem**: C strings are null-terminated. Lua strings can contain nulls. Need careful conversion.

**Solution**:
- Use `ffi.string()` for C → Lua
- Use `ffi.copy()` for Lua → C
- Always validate pointer before using

#### Challenge 4: Memory Management

**Problem**: WebUI allocates some strings. Must call `webui_free()` to avoid leaks.

**Solution**: 
- Wrap in Lua GC metamethods where possible
- Document when manual free is needed

### 5.2 Platform Challenges

#### Challenge 5: Building WebUI on Each Platform

**Problem**: Need to compile C library on Windows, macOS, Linux.

**Solution**:
- Use LuaRocks to build
- Provide prebuilt binaries in rock (if allowed)
- Document build requirements

#### Challenge 6: Browser Detection

**Problem**: Users may not have ANY browser installed (rare but possible).

**Solution**: 
```lua
if not webui.browserExist(webui.AnyBrowser) then
    error("No supported browser found. Please install Chrome, Firefox, or Edge.")
end
```

### 5.3 API Design Challenges

#### Challenge 7: Returning Values Asynchronously

**Problem**: With async events, how to return values after the callback?

**Solution**: Use event number to track:
```lua
Window:bind("btn", function(event)
    -- Store event number → callback mapping
    -- Later when async operation completes:
    -- webui_interface_set_response(window, event_number, "result")
end)
```

#### Challenge 8: Multiple Windows & Event Routing

**Problem**: Single event loop but multiple windows. Which window does an event belong to?

**Solution**: Event struct includes `window` field. Route to correct Window object:
```lua
local windows = {}  -- window_id → Window instance

Window:bind("btn", function(event)
    local win = windows[event.window]
    -- use win:returnString() or event:return()
end)
```

---

## Things To Keep In Mind

### 6.1 Performance

- **Don't** convert all strings unnecessarily in hot paths
- **Cache** FFI function pointers if calling repeatedly
- **Batch** operations when possible
- **Profile** before optimizing

### 6.2 Memory

- Always free strings returned by WebUI
- Don't keep references to C-managed memory
- Use Lua strings for long-term storage

### 6.3 Error Handling

```lua
-- Good pattern
local ok, result = pcall(function()
    return window:getUrl()
end)

if not ok then
    print("Error: " .. result)
    return
end
```

- Wrap all FFI calls in pcall initially
- Check for nil pointers before use
- Provide meaningful error messages

### 6.4 Browser Choice

- Default to `AnyBrowser` - WebUI picks best available
- Don't force specific browser unless needed
- Test on all three platforms

### 6.5 Security

- WebUI creates local server on random port
- Uses private browser profile
- Can be scoped to localhost only with `setPublic(false)`
- **Default should be: private/localhost**

### 6.6 webui.js Integration

The JavaScript bridge (`webui.js`) must be served to the browser:
```html
<script src="webui.js"></script>
```

**Options**:
1. Bundle with your app, serve from root folder
2. Download from CDN at runtime (requires network)
3. Embed in HTML (base64 or minimal version)

**Recommendation**: Copy `webui.js` into your project folder.

### 6.7 Compatibility Matrix

| Platform | Status | Notes |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Windows + LuaJIT | ✅ Primary target | Works well |
| macOS + LuaJIT | ✅ Should work | Need testing |
| Linux + LuaJIT | ✅ Should work | Need testing |
| Plain Lua 5.3+ | ❌ Not supported | FFI required |

---

## Alternatives Considered

### 7.1 Pure Lua (No FFI)

**Rejected because**:
- Can't call C library without extension
- Would require writing full HTTP/WebSocket implementation

### 7.2 C Extension Module

**Deferred** because:
- More work to maintain
- Requires compilation per platform
- FFI is sufficient and easier to distribute

### 7.3 Other GUI Libraries

| Library | Why Not |
|---------|---------|
| IUP | Old, limited styling |
| LÖVE | Game-focused, not general GUI |
| wxLua | Heavy, complex |
| PTK | Abandoned |
| NXlib | Very limited |

WebUI chosen as best balance of features, weight, and maintenance.

---

## Appendix: Reference Implementations

### Python (Best Reference)

```python
# Python WebUI - webui-dev/python-webui
from webui import webui

my_window = webui.Window()
my_window.show('<html><script src="webui.js"></script>Hello</html>')
webui.wait()
```

### Zig (OOP Reference)

```zig
// Zig WebUI - webui-dev/zig-webui  
var my_window = webui.Window.new();
try my_window.show("<html>...</html>");
webui.wait();
```

### Go (Simple Reference)

```go
// Go WebUI - webui-dev/go-webui  
myWindow := webui.NewWindow()
myWindow.Show(`<html><script src="webui.js"></script></html>`)
webui.Wait()
```

---

## Summary

This plan provides a complete roadmap for implementing a Lua WebUI binding:

1. **Why**: Lua lacks lightweight, modern GUI options
2. **How**: FFI binding to WebUI2 C library
3. **API**: Love2D-style `webui.Window.new()` 
4. **Challenges**: FFI callbacks, event parsing, memory, platform builds
5. **Timeline**: ~5 weeks for full implementation

The key pain points are manageable:
- FFI callbacks require C wrapper + registry
- Event parsing needs exact struct layouts
- Memory requires discipline with mixed Lua/C ownership

Ready to begin implementation.

# Lua-WebUI Framework - C++ Implementation Plan

## Table of Contents

1. [Executive Summary](#executive-summary)
2. [Why This Project](#why-this-project)
3. [Architecture](#architecture)
4. [Technical Architecture](#technical-architecture)
5. [API Design](#api-design)
6. [Implementation Roadmap](#implementation-roadmap)
7. [Pain Points & Challenges](#pain-points--challenges)
8. [Things To Keep In Mind](#things-to-keep-in-mind)

---

## Executive Summary

This document outlines the full implementation plan for creating a Lua/LuaJIT binding for **WebUI2** - a lightweight C library that uses the user's installed web browser as a GUI instead of bundling one.

**Target**: LuaJIT (FFI-based binding)
**License**: MIT  
**Module Name**: `webui`  
**Primary API Style**: `webui.Window.new()` (Love2D-style)

---

## Why This Project

### 1.1 The Problem

- Lua has limited native GUI options
- Existing solutions are either:
  - **Platform-specific** (IUP, Tk)
  - **Complex to embed** (Qt binding, FLTK)
  - **Heavy** (Electron wrappers)
  - **Abandoned** (many Lua GUI projects)
- No modern, lightweight, cross-platform GUI library for Lua

### 1.2 Why WebUI?

| Feature | WebUI | Electron | Qt | Tauri |
|---------|-------|----------|----|----|
| Binary Size | **~1MB** | ~150MB | ~50MB | ~10MB |
| Runtime | Browser only | Chromium + Node | Qt runtime | WebView2 |
| Portable | **Yes** | Yes | No | Yes |
| Wrapper Exists | 17+ languages | N/A | Yes | Rust only |

**Key Advantages**:
- Uses existing browser (no bundled runtime)
- Cross-platform out of the box
- Very small memory footprint
- Modern UI with HTML/CSS/JS
- Active community (4.1k GitHub stars)
- Multiple language wrappers already exist

### 1.3 Why Not Just Use Existing Lua webview?

Existing options:
- `zhaozg/lua-webview` - Uses `webview/webview.h` (different, simpler library)
- `javalikescript/lua-webview` - Also bindings to `webview/webview.h`
- `MeowBoy326/Webview-Lua` - Windows WebView2 only

**Why create new?**
- None bind to **WebUI2** (more feature-rich)
- WebUI2 has better cross-browser support
- WebUI2 includes WebSocket communication
- No complete Lua binding exists for WebUI2
- We can make it idiomatic Lua (Love2D-style API)

---

## How It Works

### 2.1 WebUI Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Your Lua Application                  │
│                                                          │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │              lua-webui Module                   │   │
│   │              (FFI Bindings)                     │   │
│   └────────────────────┬────────────────────────────┘   │
│                        │                                 │
│   ┌────────────────────▼────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │            WebUI2 C Library                      │   │
│   │         (libwebui-2.a / .so)                    │   │
│   └────────────────────┬────────────────────────────┘   │
│                        │                                 │
│              ┌────────┴────────┐                       │
│              │   WebSocket      │                       │
│              │   Connection     │                       │
│              └────────┬────────┘                       │
└────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┘
                         │
        ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐
        │                │                │
        ▼                ▼                ▼
   ┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐
   │ Chrome  │     │ Firefox │     │  Edge   │

# IPC and Frontend-Backend Communication

This document explains the inter-process communication (IPC) architecture that enables JavaScript frontend code to call Rust backend functions and receive events. It covers the `invoke()` system, message protocol, command handlers, channels for streaming data, and the event system for bidirectional communication.

For information about custom protocol handlers for serving assets, see [Custom Protocol Handlers](/tauri-apps/tauri/4.4-custom-protocol-handlers). For details on the JavaScript API package structure, see [@tauri-apps/api Package Structure](/tauri-apps/tauri/5.1-api-package-structure-and-build).

---

## IPC Architecture Overview

Tauri's IPC system bridges the JavaScript frontend (running in a webview) with the Rust backend through a message-passing protocol. The communication is bidirectional: the frontend can invoke Rust commands via `invoke()`, and the backend can emit events to the frontend.

**Sources:** [crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs1-227](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs#L1-L227) [crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs1-115](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs#L1-L115) [examples/api/src/App.svelte1-20](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/examples/api/src/App.svelte#L1-L20)

---

## IPC Protocol Implementation

### The `ipc://` Custom Protocol

Tauri implements IPC using a custom `ipc://` URI scheme protocol. When JavaScript code calls `invoke()`, the request is sent as an HTTP POST to `ipc://localhost` with the command name, arguments, and callback identifiers.

The protocol handler is registered in [crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs274-280](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs#L274-L280):

```
if !registered_scheme_protocols.contains(&"ipc".into()) {
  let protocol = crate::ipc::protocol::get(manager.manager_owned());
  pending.register_uri_scheme_protocol("ipc", move |webview_id, request, responder| {
    protocol(webview_id, request, UriSchemeResponder(responder))
  });
}

```

**Sources:** [crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs38-183](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs#L38-L183) [crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs274-280](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs#L274-L280)

### Message Structure and Headers

The IPC protocol uses specific HTTP headers to manage the request-response cycle:

| Header Name      | Direction | Purpose                                                   |
| ---------------- | --------- | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tauri-Callback   | Request   | Callback function ID for successful response              |
| Tauri-Error      | Request   | Callback function ID for error response                   |
| Tauri-Invoke-Key | Request   | Runtime-generated key to prevent unauthorized invocations |
| Tauri-Response   | Response  | Indicates "ok" or "error" status                          |
| Content-Type     | Both      | MIME type (application/json or application/octet-stream)  |

The invoke key is generated at runtime in the `AppManager` and embedded in initialization scripts to ensure only the webview can invoke commands:

**Sources:** [crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs24-30](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs#L24-L30) [crates/tauri/src/manager/mod.rs212-218](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/manager/mod.rs#L212-L218)

### Request Parsing and Deserialization

The protocol handler supports two types of message bodies:

1. **Standard JSON IPC** (post-message or custom protocol):  
   * Deserializes JSON payload directly  
   * Used for most command invocations
2. **Isolation Pattern Encrypted Payload** (when `isolation` feature is enabled):  
   * Decrypts AES-GCM encrypted payload  
   * Provides additional security layer

The deserialization logic is in [crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs185-293](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs#L185-L293):

```
#[cfg(feature = "isolation")]
if let crate::Pattern::Isolation { crypto_keys, .. } = &*manager.pattern {
  invoke_message.replace(
    serde_json::from_str::<IsolationMessage<'_>>(request.body())
      .and_then(|message| {
        let payload = crypto_keys.decrypt(message.payload)?;
        // ... create Message from decrypted payload
      })
  );
}

```

**Sources:** [crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs185-293](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs#L185-L293) [crates/tauri-utils/src/pattern/isolation.rs104-114](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri-utils/src/pattern/isolation.rs#L104-L114)

---

## Command System

### Defining Commands

Commands are Rust functions decorated with the `#[tauri::command]` procedural macro. This macro generates wrapper code that handles deserialization of arguments and serialization of return values.

Commands can accept various parameter types:

| Parameter Type  | Description                         | Example              |
| --------------- | ----------------------------------- | -------------------- |
| Primitive types | JSON-deserializable values          | String, i32, Vec<T>  |
| State<T>        | Managed application state           | State<'\_, Database> |
| AppHandle<R>    | Application handle                  | AppHandle<R>         |
| Window<R>       | Window that invoked command         | Window<R>            |
| Webview<R>      | Webview that invoked command        | Webview<R>           |
| Custom types    | Types implementing CommandArg trait | Custom deserializers |

## Invoke Response and Callbacks

### Response Types

Commands can return results in two formats:

1. **JSON Response** (`InvokeResponseBody::Json`):  
   * Serialized JSON string  
   * Content-Type: `application/json`
2. **Raw Binary Response** (`InvokeResponseBody::Raw`):  
   * Raw bytes (`Vec<u8>`)  
   * Content-Type: `application/octet-stream`

The response type is determined by the command's return type and serialization behavior.

**Sources:** [crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs112-126](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs#L112-L126)

### Callback Mechanism

When using the custom protocol IPC (as opposed to post-message), the frontend provides callback and error function IDs in the request. The backend sends the response via HTTP, and the frontend bridge calls the appropriate callback:

For post-message IPC, responses are sent by evaluating JavaScript that calls the callback functions directly:

**Sources:** [crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs48-140](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs#L48-L140) [crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs323-382](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs#L323-L382)

### Channel Creation and Usage

Tauri provides a bidirectional event system where both frontend and backend can emit and listen to events.

## Security: Invoke Key Validation

To prevent unauthorized command invocations from external scripts, Tauri generates a random invoke key at runtime and validates it on every IPC request.

### Invoke Key Generation

The key is generated when creating the `AppManager`:

**Sources:** [crates/tauri/src/manager/mod.rs212-218](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/manager/mod.rs#L212-L218) [crates/tauri/src/manager/mod.rs678-680](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/manager/mod.rs#L678-L680)

### Invoke Key Injection

The key is injected into the webview via initialization scripts:

This happens in the core initialization script defined in [crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs367-372](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs#L367-L372):

### Invoke Key Validation

Every IPC request must include the invoke key in the `Tauri-Invoke-Key` header or `__TAURI_INVOKE_KEY__` field. The protocol handler validates this before executing commands:

**Sources:** [crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs241-242](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs#L241-L242) [crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs367-399](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs#L367-L399)

---

## Initialization Scripts and JavaScript Bridge

### IPC Initialization Script Injection

Tauri injects several initialization scripts into webviews to set up the IPC bridge. These scripts run before any other JavaScript in the page.

The initialization sequence is defined in [crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs153-218](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs#L153-L218):

**Sources:** [crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs153-218](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs#L153-L218) [crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs344-399](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs#L344-L399)

### The `__TAURI_INTERNALS__` Global Object

The `__TAURI_INTERNALS__` object serves as the primary bridge between frontend and backend:

| Property  | Type   | Purpose                        |
| --------- | ------ | ------------------------------ |
| plugins   | Object | Plugin-specific API namespaces |
| metadata  | Object | Current window/webview labels  |
| invokeKey | String | Runtime-generated security key |
| callbacks | Map    | Pending callback functions     |
| channels  | Map    | Active channel instances       |

This object is populated by the initialization scripts and used by the `@tauri-apps/api` package to implement the `invoke()` function.

**Sources:** [crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs160-188](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs#L160-L188)

### Process IPC Message Function

The core IPC message processing function is defined in [crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs42-43](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs#L42-L43):

This script handles:

* Message deserialization
* Callback invocation
* Error handling
* Channel message dispatching

The function is injected as part of the invoke initialization script and integrated into the webview's message handling pipeline.

**Sources:** [crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs42-43](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs#L42-L43) [crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs189-194](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/manager/webview.rs#L189-L194)

The isolation pattern uses AES-256-GCM encryption with keys generated at runtime:

## Post-Message IPC Alternative

In addition to the custom protocol approach, Tauri supports a post-message based IPC mechanism for certain platforms or configurations.

### Post-Message Handler

The post-message handler is registered on the webview and processes messages sent via `window.postMessage()` or the webview's native message handler:

The handler is defined in [crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs185-352](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs#L185-L352):

**Sources:** [crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs32-36](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs#L32-L36) [crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs185-352](https://github.com/tauri-apps/tauri/blob/3b5b2cc1/crates/tauri/src/ipc/protocol.rs#L185-L352)

## Summary Table: IPC Communication Methods

| Method                   | Transport               | Use Case                      | Security              |
| ------------------------ | ----------------------- | ----------------------------- | --------------------- |
| Custom Protocol (ipc://) | HTTP over custom scheme | Primary IPC mechanism         | Invoke key validation |
| Post-Message             | WebView native messages | Fallback or platform-specific | Invoke key validation |
| Isolation + Encryption   | Encrypted post-message  | High-security applications    | AES-GCM + invoke key  |
| Channel Streaming        | Same as invoke method   | Long-running data streams     | Same as parent invoke |
| Events                   | JavaScript eval         | Backend-to-frontend push      | Webview sandboxing    |

Why This Architecture ### Comparison with Existing Approaches | Framework | Backend | Frontend | Binary Size | Portable | |-----------|---------|----------|-------------|----------| | **Electron** | Node.js (~50MB) | Chromium | ~150MB | Yes | | **Tauri** | Rust | WebView2/WebKit | ~3-10MB | Yes | | **Wails** | Go | WebView | ~15MB | Yes | | **coconut-milk** | C++ + LuaJIT | WebUI | ~5-15MB | Yes | ### Why Not Just FFI/Lua-only? Architecture Overview ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ coconut-milk │ │ (Your Binary) │ │ │ │ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ C++ Core Layer │ │ │ │ ┌─────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐ │ │ │ │ │ Lua Engine │ │ WebUI Lib │ │ C++ Standard │ │ │ │ │ │ (LuaJIT) │ │ (C lib) │ │ Library │ │ │ │ │ └──────┬──────┘ └──────┬──────┘ └───────┬────────┘ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ ▼ ▼ │ │ │ │ │ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ │ │ Lua ↔ C++ Bridge │ │ │ │ │ │ - Expose C++ to Lua (webui.*) │ │ │ │ │ │ - Expose Lua to C++ (callbacks) │ │ │ │ │ └─────────────────────┬────────────────────┘ │ │ │ └────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┘ │ └────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┘ │ ┌──────────────┼──────────────┐ │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ │ Chrome │ │ Firefox │ │ Edge │ └─────────┘ └─────────┘ └─────────┘ │ │ │ └──────────────┼──────────────┘ ▼ ┌─────────────────┐ │ webui.js │ │ (Browser Bridge)│ └─────────────────┘ ``` --- ## 4. IPC Communication Design Based on **Tauri** and **Wails** patterns: ### 4.1 Lua → Browser (Push) ```lua -- Lua code window:emit("update", {progress = 50}) ``` ```javascript // JavaScript window.listen("update", (event) => { console.log(event.data.progress) // 50 }) ``` ### 4.2 Browser → Lua (Commands) ```javascript // JavaScript const result = await window.invoke("greet", {name: "World"}) ``` ```lua -- Lua code function greet(args) return "Hello, " .. args.name end ``` ### 4.3 Communication Flow (detailed) ``` JavaScript C++ Engine Lua │ │ │ │ invoke("cmd", {data}) │ │ │─────────────────────────────────►│ │ │ │ Find cmd in registry │ │ │──────────────────────────►│ │ │ │ │ │ Execute Lua function │ │ │◄──────────────────────────│ │ │ │ │ Return JSON response │ │ │◄──────────────────────────────────│ │ │ │ │ ``` --- ## 5. Lua API Design ### 5.1 Module Structure ```lua local webui = require("webui") -- Constants webui.version -- "2.5.0" webui.Chrome -- browser constant webui.Firefox webui.Edge -- Create window local window = webui.newWindow() -- Show content window:show(html_string) window:showFile("index.html") -- Wait for close webui.wait() ``` ### 5.2 Full Lua API ```lua -- Global webui.newWindow() → Window webui.wait() webui.exit() webui.setConfig(option, boolean) -- Window class window:show(content) -- HTML, URL, file window:showBrowser(content, browser) window:close() window:destroy() -- Bind Lua to JavaScript triggers window:bind("elementId", function(event) local data = event:getString(0) window:returnString(event, "response") end) -- Execute JavaScript window:run("alert('hello')") local ok, result = window:script("return 1 + 1", 5) -- Events window:on("eventName", function(data) end) window:emit("eventName", {key = "value"}) -- Window control window:setSize(800, 600) window:setPosition(100, 100) window:setKiosk(true) window:focus() ``` --- ## 6. C++ Core Implementation ### 6.1 Main Components ```cpp // main.cpp #include "coconut.hpp" #include "lua_bridge.hpp" #include "webui_bridge.hpp" int main() { // Initialize CoconutApp app; // Open Lua state app.openLua(); // Load user script app.loadScript("main.lua"); // Run event loop app.run(); return 0; } ``` ### 6.2 Lua Bridge (Expose C++ to Lua) ```cpp // lua_bridge.hpp #include #include class LuaBridge { lua_State* L; public: void init(); void registerWebUI(); // Register webui.* functions void registerCFunctions(); // Custom C++ functions exposed to Lua // Helper to call Lua from C++ int callLuaFunction(const char* name, const char* args); // Helper to expose C++ to Lua template void registerFunction(const char* name, F func); }; ``` ### 6.3 WebUI Bridge (C++ wrapper for WebUI) ```cpp // webui_bridge.hpp #include #include #include struct Window { size_t id; bool is_showing; // Method implementations bool show(const char* content); void close(); void runScript(const char* script); // ... }; class WebUIBridge { std::map windows; std::map> callbacks; public: void init(); Window* newWindow(); void bind(Window* win, const char* element, std::function callback); void setResponse(size_t event_num, const char* response); }; ``` --- ## 7. JavaScript Integration ### 7.1 Injected JS Bridge The C++ app serves this to the browser via WebUI: ```javascript // coconut-bridge.js (served automatically) window.coconut = { // Invoke Lua command invoke: async function(cmd, args) { // JSON-RPC style call via WebUI const response = await webui.call(cmd, JSON.stringify(args)); return JSON.parse(response); }, // Listen for events from Lua on: function(event, callback) { webui.bind(event, function(e) { callback(JSON.parse(e.detail)); }); }, // Emit to Lua emit: function(event, data) { webui.call("__emit:" + event, JSON.stringify(data)); } }; // Quick alias const invoke = window.coconut.invoke; const on = window.coconut.on; const emit = window.coconut.emit; ``` ### 7.2 User's Frontend Code ```html
``` --- ## 8. Pain Points & Solutions ### 8.1 Embedding Lua in C++ **Challenge**: The Lua C API is low-level and verbose. **Solution**: - Use LuaJIT's FFI-friendly headers - Create C++ wrapper classes around lua_State - Use luaL_openlibs() for standard libraries ```cpp // Simple example lua_State* L = luaL_newstate(); luaL_openlibs(L); luaL_dostring(L, "print('Hello from Lua!')"); lua_close(L); ``` ### 8.2 Exposing C++ Functions to Lua **Challenge**: Need to bridge C++ functions to Lua callable. **Solution**: - Use lua_CFunction adapter - Register in lua_Reg array ```cpp // C++ function int cpp_greet(lua_State* L) { const char* name = luaL_checkstring(L, 1); std::string result = "Hello, " + std::string(name); lua_pushstring(L, result.c_str()); return 1; } // Registration static const luaL_Reg funcs[] = { {"greet", cpp_greet}, {NULL, NULL} }; ``` ### 8.3 Lua Callbacks in C++ (C++ calling Lua) **Challenge**: WebUI callbacks happen in C, need to call Lua. **Solution**: - Store Lua function reference (luaL_ref) - When C callback fires, pcall the Lua function ```cpp void webui_callback(webui_event_t* e) { // Get Lua function from registry lua_rawgeti(L, LUA_REGISTRYINDEX, callback_ref); lua_pushstring(L, e->element); lua_pcall(L, 1, 0, 0); } ``` ### 8.4 Serialization Between Lua ↔ JS **Challenge**: Different data types. **Solution**: Use JSON as lingua franca - Lua → JSON via cjson or manual - JavaScript → JSON via JSON.stringify ```lua -- Lua side local json = require("cjson") local data = json.encode({name = "world"}) local parsed = json.decode('{"name":"world"}') ``` ### 8.5 Building for Multiple Platforms **Challenge**: C++ on Windows/macOS/Linux. **Solution**: - Use CMake - Conditional compilation - Bundle WebUI lib + Lua statically --- ## 9. Implementation Phases ### Phase 1: C++ Shell - [x] Create CMake project - [ ] Build static WebUI library - [ ] Embed LuaJIT - [ ] Basic main() → window opens ### Phase 2: Lua Integration - [ ] Expose basic print() to Lua - [ ] Execute Lua script from C++ - [ ] Create webui.* Lua module ### Phase 3: Window Management - [ ] Implement Window:show() - [ ] Implement webui.wait() - [ ] Test HTML display ### Phase 4: IPC System - [ ] Implement window:bind() - JS calls Lua - [ ] Implement window:emit() - Lua calls JS - [ ] Serialize arguments properly ### Phase 5: Polish - [ ] Error handling - [ ] Documentation - [ ] Examples - [ ] Build scripts for platforms --- ## 10. File Structure ``` coconut-milk/ ├── CMakeLists.txt # Build system ├── src/ │ ├── main.cpp # Entry point │ ├── coconut.hpp # App wrapper │ ├── coconut.cpp │ ├── lua_bridge.hpp # Lua ↔ C++ binding │ ├── lua_bridge.cpp │ ├── webui_bridge.hpp # WebUI C++ wrapper │ ├── webui_bridge.cpp │ ├── lua/ │ │ └── webui.lua # Lua module │ └── js/ │ ├── coconut-bridge.js # JS bridge (served) │ └── webui.js # From WebUI project ├── examples/ │ ├── 01_hello/ │ │ ├── main.lua │ │ └── index.html │ ├── 02_button/ │ │ ├── main.lua │ │ └── index.html │ └── 03_events/ │ ├── main.lua │ └── index.html ├── libs/ │ ├── webui/ # WebUI source (submodule) │ └── lua/ # LuaJIT source (or prebuilt) └── .github/ └── workflows/ └── build.yml ``` --- ## Summary This plan creates something like **Electron** but: 1. **Backend**: C++ instead of Node.js 2. **Scripting**: Lua instead of JavaScript 3. **GUI**: WebUI (browser-based) instead of Chromium embedded 4. **Distribution**: Single portable binary The key insight from researching Tauri/Wails: - Use **JSON** for communication - Expose **commands** that Lua can register - Use **events** for push notifications - Embed **bridge JS** that gets served to browser This is significantly more robust than the FFI approach because you control the entire stack and can distribute as one binary.
